sentences.text: Survivor is John Lowy.
Interviewer is Libby Raichman.
It is Sydney, Australia.
The language is English and this is tape 1.
I was born in 8th of May, 1925.
I will be 71 next month, eight of May.
I was born then Czechoslovakia, Fil'akovo.
Yes.
It was a rather small, dirty town.
It was-- 5,000 inhabitants.
There was a-- a large industrial undertaking there, which they were manufacturing household or kitchen goods, stoves, ovens, baths, anything which is from metal.
And the population was 5,000.
And 5,000 people worked in the factory.
Yes.
But obviously, a lot of them came from surrounding areas as well.
Eight-- I think it's 45.
There was 45 Jewish families taken, a few families which lived in the surrounding villages.
Yes.
Actually, they belonged to the Jewish congregation, members of the Jewish congregation, if you like.
Yes we did.
Yes.
Sorry?
I would say 90% would be observant.
I mean, there's a grade of observancy, like in every community.
There was very strict and a little bit more loose.
But quite religious.
Yes.
I think we had [NON-ENGLISH].
It was-- it had a cheder.
And other religious facilities for guidance, marriages, bris, and the usual sort of thing.
Yes.
We did have a rabbi who was very respected.
I mean, I saw him.
Very old, and he was very well respected.
His name was Rabbi Jungreis, which comes from a very large rabbinical family.
There was quite a few Jungreises in Hungary or in Czechoslovakia.
No.
The cheder, they engaged a teacher who was somehow qualified.
But as quite a few of the congregants, they went broke, especially during the Depression.
And to re-establish themselves, it wasn't very easy.
So they could pray.
They weren't qualified teachers.
But they could pray.
They were religious.
So they gave them some sort of a job, to teach the children in cheder.
OK.
But later, I think when I was starting to go to cheder, they engaged a qualified Jewish teacher.
He came from Northern Slovakia, from [NON-ENGLISH], Barajavo.
And he was quite intelligent, learned in secular things as well.
So our education has improved somewhat.
And as we did not speak Yiddish at all.
I mean, the second language, commercial and cultural language, was German.
And he was very well equipped with German knowledge.
So we translated the [HEBREW] from Hebrew, from-- from the writing into a German translation.
Instead of Yiddish.
And also, he taught us German grammar and to write in German, with the Gothic writing.
So it was quite a-- a-- a change from the past which we had.
So I somehow either must have been lucky that I got that part of-- of a education in it, back at home.
Otherwise, our education was mainly self education.
There was a lot of reading.
And whatever you can sort of put your hands on, you read.
I mean, newspaper, magazines, and lots of books.
I was not-- Were they easily available?
Pardon?
It was.
I mean, either you borrowed it or you bought it.
And sort of everything which was turning around-- around in the house, children were always involved.
So we were in quite sort of a-- we started to live a life of reality.
I mean, what sort of a reality it was.
And we were being part of practically everything which happened in our home, and also in the community.
Can you tell me what Fil'akovo looked like, where it was situated?
Mountainous, flat?
Did it have a river?
What--
Fil'akovo is, from an aesthetic point of view, was really very bad.
It had a-- a historical background.
There was a ruin of a castle which was the middle of the town, which was the focus of our sort of recreation at the time.
Because if you wanted to go somewhere, then we went up to that ruined castle.
We played there.
So that gave us some sort of a feel of-- of interest.
Or it was--
A sense of history, I suppose.
Yes, a sense of history.
But otherwise, it was a-- a dirty town.
There was no paved roads or-- or footpaths.
And it was-- during the rainy days, you went into a-- a mud up to your knees, practically.
And then in summer, when one or two cars passed sometimes.
So they brought up such a big dust that you really had to sort of take cover.
Prior to the war?
Yes.
I think we went frequently to mother's part of the family.
As my mother comes from up-- from north, where we lived.
It was in the mountains in Slovakia.
And--
The place was Revuca.
Yes.
Would you like me to spell it?
Yeah.
And that sort of was something which we always looked forward to.
Because they were beautiful people, very good relatives.
We felt always welcome.
And we spent some marvelous time up there.
Because the school holidays in that area, there was two months of the year, which was July, August.
And as long as-- as far as I remember, till 1938, we practically spent two months up there.
Which a lot of love and attention and--
It was good.
And it as beautiful there, also.
Yes, it's very nice, sort of, I remember.
Now that I'm talking about it, everything comes so beautifully.
And it was.
Family background?
Our old family, right?
Yeah.
As far as I know, I mean, my father was born in 19-- in 1894.
He was recruited to the Austria-Hungarian Army at the outbreak of the First World War.
And for four years, he went through practically all actions in front, far from Russia, head north, down to Italy, [INAUDIBLE], to the south.
So he had to take a big part of that.
And-- and I think he was always in a fighting unit.
My mother, that was brought up in more of a close, secure family surrounding, up in the mountains in Slovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The grandfather and the grandmother, they established a nice sort of a business there.
Financially, they were quite secure.
So my mother brought-- was brought up in a very protected, loving, stable, nice family.
And they had always, sort of-- I don't think they had ever, any financial problems.
And it just--
From my father's part?
Mother's part?
I mean, actually, he did this.
He came from Poland, from up north somewheres through to-- and he found himself in that area where my mother lived.
So he was a boot maker.
And then he met my grandmother and established a family, and established a business.
Obviously, business was everything.
There was only one business.
They sold everything there, you see?
There was-- couldn't-- I don't think two shops could have survived.
So-- but his main article to sell was leather sole.
Because that's what he really knew.
And they build up a very solid business, first in-- outside Revuca, which was up in the mountains.
And then to improve their-- their lifestyle, they came down to Revuca itself.
It was a fairly-- at that time, sort of a large sort of a-- a town.
And they had-- they had a high school and-- and more facilities.
And I think in the whole place, there would be about 20 Jewish families who lived.
You see, everywhere, the Jewish community was rather small and very close.
And so that was the big-- now, then--
Pardon?
Listen-- [SIGHS] Just look at me, and I said everything.
[LAUGHTER] When my mother is mentioned, you know, it's just-- I mean, I think she was the greatest, the sweetest, and the loveliest and the most intelligent.
Everything which is nice in the world, I think, in my eye, mother was.
So was my father.
I think we learned quite-- well, you see-- or actually, our happiness, if we wanted to be happy.
And the only happiness which was really sincere and was caused in that area all came from parents.
Care, love-- What--
--companionship.
If I may, you asked me about my father and mother.
So I will possibly tell you that father, when he came, he was-- he left the army.
Obviously, there was-- there was nothing he has got, I mean, after four years of fighting.
He was handsome, outgoing, love of life.
And this started people to talk.
I mean, he was, I think, 24.
My mother was 20.
I mean, it's time to get married.
So there was this matchmaker business going.
And everybody was sort of, he's got a daughter.
And I think mother received about six or eight sort of propositions that came.
And my father got there too.
And according to my mother, when my father stepped in at the door, she said, he is going to be my husband.
So it was-- so I can say it was a love affair.
But in a different way, like this, today's.
Because just before my mother passed away, she showed me a bundle of letters, which was-- there was a pink ribbon.
And they were the love letters which my father was writing to her.
So it was something, you know, which it's so good to remember.
Because this is all emotion.
This is all genuine.
This is sort of-- they're very close to my heart.
Sorry?
Yeah.
So my father got married.
And obviously, with marriage, dowry comes as well.
Because he-- he didn't have any money.
And my mother's family was reasonably set up.
So as they were-- I think they divide it somehow, how the dowry went, with so many children and so much they've got.
So when the first daughter or any daughter leaves, they get their part.
And then everything is settled.
And should the time come that the parents are retiring or they pass away, so the other-- other part as left with the boys.
So my mother really was paid out this way.
As a dowry.
As a dowry, right?
And they went back to my father's place where we lived, in Fil'akovo.
And as my father had no profession, they opened a haberdashery shop.
And they managed it quite well and-- till the Depression came.
There was already three children.
It was Alex, my eldest brother, Edith, my sister, and myself.
Father went to buy goods in the normal way because he knew so much goods will be sort of sold.
And when he came home, I mean, the situation became worse.
And they got a quite difficult financial squeeze.
Anything that had happened at that time, they always went to their own father to ask advice.
And my father went to grandfather, who was-- basically, his profession was a teacher.
So he didn't have much experience or knowledge about business-- and told that-- what the difficulties they're experiencing.
And he would have to go bankrupt, insolvent.
So they advise that my grandfather gave to my father said, my son.
You have to pull yourself together a little bit.
Don't spend so much.
Save and pay what you own.
But a Lowy will never go bankrupt or insolvent.
So obviously, they carried on for a while.
But in the end, they lost everything.
They had no money.
Nothing, no house, no anything.
Three children.
So as the family, as always, was very supportive.
I mean, from both sides.
But especially from mother's part, it was very close.
So they saw the problems.
So they came.
I mean, I don't remember much because I think I was about three years old then.
So they decided they got to live somewhere.
They got to eat something.
They have something-- something above their head, a roof.
And so my mother with father and three children went back to their parents' place.
The parents had a large house and a garden.
And there was an outer house, which was used for storage.
And--
So they've-- --have-- sort of had-- they refurbished it and made-- made it livable.
And we moved back to my mother's place-- mother's parents' place for awhile.
I just can't even imagine how they felt, you know, that they've got everything and they shared already, and now are back to where it was, with nothing.
But religious people and family people, they always have something, that God will help, that the parents will help.
So you know, they never sort of-- I mean, obviously, they-- they pulled it through.
And as children, we-- we were very happy.
Because we got so much attention, so much love, living in grandfather's place and sort of with the sisters and everything, we were really taken care of very well.
And towards the-- coming closer to the '30s, I think it must have been in 1929, things had started to improve, I mean, economically.
And my father's brother was in Fil'akovo.
He has a-- it as quite a substantial business.
He was the general agent of that factory.
So every product which went through that factory went through him.
So he had a lot of staff and-- and-- and commercial travelers.
Now that the situation has improved, so the elder brother employed my father on that account.
We came back to Fil'akovo again.
And there was house.
We rented a house, and sort of we started life all over again.
I mean, I'd just like to mention to you that outside my father, another two brothers were employed.
So you know, there was-- nobody was let loose.
I mean, if anything-- I mean, the family sort of kept very well together.
They all supported one another.
Yeah, supported.
And soon after they came-- we came back to Fil'akovo in 1930, then Frank was born.
He was really out of line.
Because there was Alex was born in 1921.
Edith was born in 1923.
I was born in 1925.
And then nothing until Frank came.
[LAUGHTER]
And that is--
Yeah.
And he was really apple of everybody's eye.
You know?
I mean, we'd all been, more or less, gone.
And so the way it was, quite a sensation.
And I think that sensation is what's keeping [INAUDIBLE] all the time.
[LAUGHTER]
Right.
CREW: ?] [?
New ?] [? angle. ?]
So life was still very difficult.
Because when you've got four children and you start again from nothing, my mother was quite ambitious.
She was always at home.
But while she was at home, she was never involved in housework, kitchen.
She was so much part of the grandparents' business.
So she was-- wanted to open a business.
But for that one, you need money again.
And you need sort of a house.
And there was a house for sale in [?
Railway ?] [?
Street ?] in Fil'akovo, which had a closed-down shop.
So my mother idea was that-- to buy that house and reopen the shop.
And she will manage it.
Father is a commercial traveler.
And mother sort of-- the two of them get together.
They were easily much-- it will become much easier to go forward.
So then again, the brothers from mother's part came into the pictures.
I think they even gave the deposit as a loan.
And they signed the guarantee of the mortgage.
Which, of course, was met regularly.
And the business which my mother was managing, it wasn't sort of a great business.
It was very hard work.
And the margin of her-- sort of-- she had a mixed business, anything which is with food.
And also, she had a restricted-- restricted liquor license, which was-- that was quite profitable.
So-- and-- and not only they worked.
Well, the one-- the way we're growing up, we'd been all working.
I mean, we-- we all was part of the action, whichever way.
For good or bad or work, I mean, it was sort of a combined effort.
And of course, 1938, when Czechoslovakia was practically crumbled to pieces, that part where we lived, it became Hungary in 1938, in October.
And it's about a 10-kilometer difference up north where my mother's family lived.
They were cut off from us.
Because that wasn't already-- it was quite a trauma that it was-- we were being cut off from the-- from the other part of the family.
But we always had hope.
I mean, we knew some of the things, which passing, and it might-- might get better.
And we're continuously listening to news and-- and reading newspaper.
And there are always, you know, there's something, news, came.
Pardon?
Of course.
I mean, we all see it, as the children too.
I mean, it was in the air.
But some people closed their eyes to it.
That's why I--
Yes.
But we couldn't.
Because everything was in front of us.
We saw it.
Sorry?
I mean, yes.
He-- yes.
Yes.
I mean, this is another thing.
You see we-- we missed our father very much.
But because of his profession, I mean, he was-- or his business, he was a commercial traveler.
He left every Monday morning.
And he came back Thursday night.
And then Friday, he sort of fixed whatever sort of-- he was done.
And then came Shabbat.
And Sunday, and he left Monday again.
I mean, he-- he traveled-- I mean, when they have to introduce-- you see, there are seasons.
I think there was two seasons when they introduced new product.
Then my uncle, you know, he was employed.
He had two cars.
So they put the new samples into a car.
And he was taking two weeks, car, traveling around Slovakia.
Otherwise, he was traveling by train.
Oh yes.
And it was-- I think my father's social life was-- mainly was in-- in the train.
Because Monday morning, there's about a dozen or two dozen commercial travelers from all the area, you know, they got together.
And they play cards.
And they davened.
And, you know, everything.
So in a way became a-- quite a social thing for them, yes.
The train was a way of life.
Yeah.
They davened on the train as well.
Yes, of course.
What?
I mean, what schooling it was.
Yes.
Yes.
[LAUGHTER]
I mean, you see, I have to sort of say, there was quite a big difference in Czechoslovakia, between regions.
And Slovakia was sort of-- neglected, rather.
And Bohemia was more advanced.
So being in Czechoslovakia, I think there was-- you know, it wasn't sufficient government support into this area.
Especially when it was a small community and the community itself wasn't very well off.
So we had a-- a state public school.
We had, actually, two schools in Fil'akovo.
Because the-- the majority of the population were Hungarian.
Actually, practically all of them were Hungarian.
So being a democratic society or state, so there was a Hungarian school and there was a Slovakian school.
We never considered Hungarian school for two reasons.
First of all, we wanted to be part of the country, so Slovakian.
And the Hungarian school was only attended by gypsy kids.
And those people were not really not interested to advance in their education.
And that was much easier for them.
And we picked the one which actually wanted to create a sort of a career for ourself.
So we-- we started in Slovakian.
But I think that the strengths of the teaching, this was very weak.
I-- I found myself that I was really, most of the time, I was bored.
Because I learned much faster than the others, as well, I think most of the Jewish children.
But-- so we had no choice, right?
So that's what it was, I mean.
The problem came, really, when you reach a stage to go to the high school.
Then you had to leave town.
Was there no high school?
There was no high school in our place.
And the compulsory elementary school was four years.
But 4 and 6 is 10 years. 10 years, child is still very little.
So it's very hard to let them go lose out of their parents' sort of supervision.
So we had five years.
And the class, it really was very boring.
But we-- that was our life.
I mean, we couldn't do very much about it.
We didn't have-- especially children.
So the next town was Lucenec, where there was a high school, two high schools, actually.
There was one which was in the lower grade, the other one.
And most of the people went to school, to Lucenec, because it was 15 kilometers by train.
And-- [SIGHS] --my parents-- well, I say our parents-- would never agree to this arrangement, that we should travel into Lucenec, 15 kilometers.
Because we have to get up early in the morning.
And in the morning, we had to pray.
And when you got bar mitzvahed, you have to put the tefillin on.
And what happens if you happen to sleep in?
Then you-- either you don't pray, you don't pray that-- you turn to put the tefillin on.
And then you go to-- to school.
So this-- it-- the religion will loosen up.
And you are-- I mean, when you are there, you might forget that something is not kosher and you eat it.
So that was out of question from our part.
I was begging.
I was asking.
